name: Kangra Valley Tea, Himachal Pradesh, India (~1290m)

staple_foods.name: green tea (Camellia sinensis)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 12.69-21.24 g/100g dry leaf total catechins (5-catechin HPLC seasonal range April–August, Wah Tea Estate, 1290m — Sourabh et al. 2013 Braz J Microbiol 44:709, PMC3910178; annual mean ~16.5 g/100g)
key_flavor_bioactives.caffeine_concentration: 2.5-3.5 g/100g dry leaf [Kangra green tea caffeine; Kangra teas typically 2–4% caffeine dry weight]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 0.8-1.5 g/100g dry leaf as theanine [mid-altitude Indian tea theanine content]

staple_foods.cultivar: Camellia sinensis (cultivar not specified; Kangra region uses both var. sinensis and assamica hybrids)
staple_foods.notes: Wah Tea Estate, Rajpura, Palampur, Kangra district, Himachal Pradesh, India. Altitude 1290m (GPS: 32°27′15.68″N, 76°31′42.26″E). Catechin data from Sourabh et al. (2013): monthly HPLC of 5 catechins (C, EC, EGC, EGCG, ECG) in processed green tea over 7 months (April–October). Individual month sums (mg/g dry): April 126.9, May 133.5, June 201.5, July 145.7, August 212.4, September 197.6, October 139.2 → annual mean 165.3 mg/g = 16.5 g/100g. EGCG dominates (>50% of total). Peak season (August) gives 21.24 g/100g; early spring (April) gives 12.69 g/100g. For regression purposes the midpoint of the full seasonal range (16.97 g/100g) is used. Kangra is one of India's oldest tea-growing regions, producing a delicate orthodox green tea distinct from Assam CTC teas. No shade cultivation (not a confound). Mid-altitude anchor between lowland Longjing (RSU-62, 100m) and the Darjeeling-Nilgiri-Kericho cluster at 1800–2200m.

notes: Kangra Valley, Himachal Pradesh, India. Located in the outer Himalayan foothills at ~1290m, Kangra is one of India's most northerly tea-growing districts. The Wah Tea Estate at Palampur is the primary research site for Sourabh et al. (2013). High rainfall (~2500mm/yr) from monsoon. Tea catechin content shows strong seasonal variation peaking in June–August (monsoon flush). Compared to Darjeeling (RSU-20, 2000m) and Assam (RSU-21, 100m), Kangra sits at an intermediate altitude with intermediate catechin content consistent with the altitude gradient. The Sourabh 2013 paper (Brazilian Journal of Microbiology) provides one of the few peer-reviewed HPLC monthly datasets for Indian highland green tea.
data_sources: Sourabh A et al. (2013) — Seasonal influence on catechin content in green tea. Brazilian Journal of Microbiology 44(3):709-715. doi:10.1590/s1517-83822013000300007. PMC3910178.
